metadata.title: One Hour on Claude Cowork
metadata.purpose: Six-wedge sixty-minute setup plan for Claude Cowork. Liam in for Bear. Covers folder creation, global instructions, project scoping, markdown files as context, first task prompt, and plugins/connectors/schedule. Notes the internet bug in the going-around version and fixes it on the way.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — the master clock; every beat conforms to its measured audio
sources: Ruben Hassid, How to AI (how-to-ai.guide) — markdown-files-as-context framing

beats.narration_text: The sixty-minute pie and its fifteen-minute context wedge is where you say people underinvest, so that's where I want to go deeper. For my own setup: (1) show me what one good markdown context file — who I am, how I write, what I never want — actually contains, (2) write the folder-protocol line in Global Instructions that marks which subfolders are read-only versus writable, and (3) explain why scoped per-project memory beats one global blob.

props.command: The sixty-minute pie and its fifteen-minute context wedge is where you say people underinvest, so that's where I want to go deeper. For my own setup: (1) show me what one good markdown context file — who I am, how I write, what I never want — actually contains, (2) write the folder-protocol line in Global Instructions that marks which subfolders are read-only versus writable, and (3) explain why scoped per-project memory beats one global blob.

beats.narration_text: The plan is a sixty-minute pie. Six wedges. Ten, ten, ten, fifteen, five, ten. Every wedge is a real task with a real payoff. The clock moves as you do. Let us start at the top.

Ten minutes. One folder on your machine called Claude Cowork. Four subfolders inside it: About Me, Templates, Projects, Claude Outputs. That is the whole physical setup. The trade-off, said plainly: Cowork gets real read and write access to whatever is inside this folder. That is the superpower and the risk in one sentence. Keep the folder tight, because everything in it is fair game.

Ten more minutes. Settings, then Cowork, then Edit Global Instructions. This is the rulebook Claude follows on every task before you type a single word. The line that earns its keep: the folder protocol. Which folders are read-only, which are writable. That one line is the difference between an assistant and a loose cannon.

Another ten minutes. Import a project or point Cowork at an existing folder. Each project gets its own folder, its own custom instructions, its own scheduled tasks. The real unlock is scoped memory: Claude remembers what it did inside this project only. Not one global blob. A container. That scoping is the thing that makes projects useful instead of confusing.

Fifteen minutes. This is the biggest wedge, and where people underinvest the most. Context as files beats context as prompting. One good markdown file — who you are, how you write, what you never want — is worth more than a pile of random uploads. The more of your context lives as structured files, the less you re-explain every session. That framing comes from Ruben Hassid at How to AI, and it is correct.

Five minutes. The highest-leverage first prompt is not a command. It is: I want to do this task. Ask me questions first. Claude turns that into clickable forms instead of guessing. And for the model: pick the most capable one available and turn Extended Thinking on. No version numbers in this video — version numbers rot. The principle does not.

Last ten minutes. Official plugins cover Sales, Marketing, Legal, Finance. Type slash for slash commands. Wire up your connectors. Set tasks to run on a schedule. You can customize a plugin for your own company so the slash commands speak your language. That is the sixty-minute clock. Full rotation.
Let's recap with Claude. An hour of setup buys you months of not re-explaining yourself. The machine does the work. You keep the judgment. And now it knows where your files live. That is the deal.

artifactLines: An hour of setup buys you months of not re-explaining yourself.
The machine does the work. You keep the judgment.
It knows where your files live.

beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, drop it into Claude Cowork, and it will walk you through the setup one question at a time. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: I want to set up my Cowork workspace. Ask me questions first.
output: walks you through the whole hour

props.title: One Hour on Claude Cowork
props.subline: infographic: Ruben Hassid — How to AI (how-to-ai.guide)
